text: Patient's position is supine with reverse Trendelenburg and tilt left down. Port sites placement and robotic setup are as shown in the pictures.

The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures involved in the procedure, followed by extended cauterization of the duodenum, including the transverse third portion and pancreatic head, to achieve good exposure of the diverticulum.

The surgeon has fully mobilized the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava and left renal vein. The next step involves identifying and dissecting the duodenal diverticulum, which appears as a herniation of the duodenal mucosa through a muscle defect.

The surgeon carefully dissects toward the duodenal wall, identifying the neck of the diverticulum between separated muscle layers. The diverticulum is then transected at the neck using a laparoscopic linear stapler or cautery, followed by primary closure.
The surgeon approximates the duodenal wall defect over the diverticular stump, securing the closure. The specimen is placed into an endo bag and retrieved through the assistant port.
